walkthrough.title: Meet the Critters
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Cockroach Poker! Let's get this bluffing party started. First, find the deck of sixty-four cards. There are eight types of creepy critters in the deck: Cockroach, Bat, Fly, Toad, Rat, Scorpion, Spider, and Stink Bug. Each type has exactly eight cards, and every card shows one big gross critter on the front.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Deck
walkthrough.text: Give the entire deck a thorough shuffle. You want these critters nice and randomized. This is the only deck in the game — there are no boards, no tokens, no score tracks. Just sixty-four cards and your poker face.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal the entire deck out evenly to all players. Everyone gets the same number of cards. With five players, that is twelve cards each with four left over — just set the extras aside face-down without looking at them. Hold your cards as a hand so only you can see them.

walkthrough.title: Choose Who Goes First
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first however you like. The youngest player, the bravest liar, or whoever touched a bug most recently. That player will make the first offer. There is no turn order after that — whoever gets stuck with a face-up card becomes the next offerer. Let's get bluffing!

summary.title: Meet the Critters
bullets: 64 cards total, 8 critter types with 8 cards each
Types: Cockroach, Bat, Fly, Toad, Rat, Scorpion, Spider, Stink Bug

summary.title: Shuffle the Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 64 cards together
No boards, tokens, or score tracks — just cards

summary.title: Deal the Cards
bullets: Deal entire deck evenly to all players
Set aside any extras face-down without looking
Hold cards as a hidden hand

summary.title: Choose Who Goes First
bullets: Pick any method to choose first player
First player makes the opening offer
No fixed turn order — loser of each round offers next

walkthrough.title: The Goal: Don't Lose
walkthrough.text: Cockroach Poker is not about winning — it is about not losing! There is no scoring and no points. The game keeps going until one player loses, and then everyone else wins together. You lose if you collect four face-up cards of the same critter type in front of you, or if you have to make an offer but your hand is completely empty.

walkthrough.title: Making an Offer
walkthrough.text: On your turn, pick any card from your hand and place it face-down in front of another player. As you slide it over, you must claim what it is by naming one of the eight critter types. You might say 'This is a Cockroach' or 'This is a Bat.' Here is the fun part — you can be telling the truth or you can be lying through your teeth. It is entirely up to you.

walkthrough.title: Responding: Guess or Peek
walkthrough.text: When someone offers you a card, you have two choices. Choice one: you guess. Say 'Yes, it is' if you think they are telling the truth, or 'No, it is not' if you think they are bluffing. Choice two: you peek at the card secretly without showing anyone else, and then pass it to a different player with a new or same claim. You cannot pass it back to anyone who has already seen that card this round.

walkthrough.title: Resolving a Guess
walkthrough.text: When someone guesses, flip the card face-up for everyone to see. If the guesser was right — they called the bluff correctly or confirmed the truth — the person who offered the card must place it face-up in front of themselves. If the guesser was wrong, they keep the card face-up in front of themselves. The loser of that exchange is the one who makes the next offer.

walkthrough.title: Peeking and Passing
walkthrough.text: If you choose to peek instead of guessing, secretly look at the card. Now you know exactly what it is! Slide it face-down to any other player who has not seen this card yet, and make a new claim. You can say the same critter type the previous person claimed, or you can change it to something completely different. The new recipient now faces the same choice: guess or peek and pass.

walkthrough.title: Forced to Guess
walkthrough.text: A card can only be peeked and passed so many times. If every other player at the table has already seen that card during this round, the last player receiving it has no one left to pass to. That player must guess — there is no peeking option. This is where things get spicy because everyone else at the table knows what the card is except potentially the last person in the chain.

walkthrough.title: Face-Up Cards and Losing
walkthrough.text: Every time you lose a guess, the card goes face-up in front of you for the rest of the game. These cards are public — everyone can see what critters are piling up in front of you. The moment any player has four face-up cards of the same type, that player loses and the game is over. You also lose instantly if it is your turn to offer but you have zero cards in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Quick Recap
walkthrough.text: The offerer picks a card, places it face-down, and claims a critter. The recipient either guesses or peeks and passes. If they guess, the loser keeps the card face-up and offers next. If they peek, they pass it along with a claim. The game ends when someone has four of a kind face-up or an empty hand on their offer turn. Simple rules, infinite mind games!

summary.title: The Goal: Don't Lose
bullets: No scoring — one player loses, everyone else wins
Lose if: 4 face-up cards of the same critter type
Lose if: must offer but hand is empty

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Cockroach Poker Practice
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a practice game with five players — Alex, Bailey, Casey, Dana, and Eli. We will play out several rounds so you can see offering, guessing, peeking, passing, and the mistakes new players make. Follow along and you will be a bluffing pro in no time!

walkthrough.title: Round One: Alex Offers
walkthrough.text: Alex looks at their hand and picks a Cockroach card. They slide it face-down to Bailey and announce 'This is a Cockroach.' Alex is telling the truth this time — a bold opening move. Bailey has to decide: do they believe Alex or call the bluff?

walkthrough.title: Bailey Guesses Wrong
walkthrough.text: Bailey thinks Alex is bluffing and says 'No, it is not a Cockroach.' They flip the card and — surprise — it really is a Cockroach! Bailey guessed wrong, so the Cockroach goes face-up in front of Bailey. That is one Cockroach down, three more before Bailey is in real trouble. Bailey now has to make the next offer.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Bailey Offers
walkthrough.text: Bailey picks a Rat card from their hand and slides it face-down to Casey. Bailey says 'This is a Spider.' That is a lie — it is actually a Rat. Casey looks at Bailey, trying to read their expression.

walkthrough.title: Casey Peeks and Passes
walkthrough.text: Casey decides not to risk guessing. Instead, Casey peeks at the card secretly — it is a Rat, not a Spider like Bailey claimed. Now Casey slides the card face-down to Dana and says 'This is a Rat.' Casey is telling the truth because they know exactly what it is. Smart play — Casey turned the tables by gaining information.

walkthrough.title: Dana Guesses Right
walkthrough.text: Dana thinks for a moment. Casey peeked at the card and had no reason to lie about it — the pressure is on Bailey, not Casey. Dana says 'Yes, it is a Rat.' They flip the card, and it is indeed a Rat! Dana was right, so Bailey, who originally offered the card, has to take it face-up. Bailey now has a Cockroach and a Rat in front of them.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Number One: Always Lying
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Bailey make a classic beginner mistake. Bailey offers three more cards in a row and lies about every single one. The other players catch on fast. By the third offer, everyone just says 'No it is not' and they are right every time. Bailey racks up face-up cards quickly. The lesson: if you always lie, you become predictable. Mix truth and lies to keep people guessing!

walkthrough.title: The Board State Heats Up
walkthrough.text: After several rounds, here is where everyone stands. Bailey has three Cockroaches and one Rat face-up — one more Cockroach and Bailey is out! Dana has two Flies and a Scorpion. Everyone else has one or two scattered cards. The table tension is rising because Bailey is one card away from losing.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Number Two: Ignoring the Danger Zone
walkthrough.text: Eli has a Cockroach in hand and needs to offer it to someone. The obvious target is Bailey, who already has three Cockroaches face-up. But Eli completely ignores this and offers it to Alex instead, claiming 'This is a Fly.' Alex easily guesses 'No it is not' because the claim felt random. Now Eli is stuck with the card face-up. The lesson: always check who is close to losing. Targeting the player with three of a type forces them into a terrifying guess!

walkthrough.title: A Better Play: Targeting Weakness
walkthrough.text: Let's rewind that. The smart move for Eli would be to slide that Cockroach face-down to Bailey and say 'This is a Cockroach.' Now Bailey is sweating. If Bailey believes Eli and says 'Yes it is' but it actually is a Cockroach, Bailey takes it and loses! But if Bailey says 'No it is not' and it really is a Cockroach, Bailey also loses. The psychological pressure is immense when someone already has three of a type.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Number Three: Never Peeking
walkthrough.text: Alex receives an offer from Dana and immediately guesses without thinking. Wrong! Alex takes another card face-up. This keeps happening — Alex always guesses on the spot and never peeks. The lesson: peeking is incredibly powerful. When you peek, you gain perfect information about the card. You can pass it along truthfully, lie about it, or target someone who is vulnerable. Guessing blind is a coin flip, but peeking turns you into the one with the advantage.

walkthrough.title: The Final Showdown
walkthrough.text: Casey offers Bailey a card face-down and says 'This is a Cockroach.' Bailey has three Cockroaches face-up already and cannot afford to get this wrong. Bailey decides to peek instead of guessing — and sees it is actually a Bat! Bailey passes it to Eli and says 'This is a Bat.' Eli believes Bailey and says 'Yes it is a Bat.' The card flips — it is a Bat. Eli was right! Casey, the original offerer, takes the Bat face-up. Bailey survives another round!

walkthrough.title: And the Loser Is...
walkthrough.text: A few rounds later, Dana offers Bailey a card claiming it is a Toad. Bailey peeks — it is a Cockroach! Bailey passes it to Eli saying 'This is a Scorpion.' Eli peeks and sees it is a Cockroach. The only player left who has not seen this card is Alex. Eli passes to Alex and says 'This is a Cockroach' — telling the truth. Alex must guess because everyone else has seen the card. Alex says 'No it is not.' They flip it. Cockroach! Alex was wrong and takes it face-up. The chain of peeking kept Bailey alive!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Great job following along! You have seen the core flow of Cockroach Poker in action — offering, guessing, peeking, passing, and the three big mistakes to avoid. Remember: mix up your truth and lies so you are not predictable, always target players who are close to four of a kind, and use peeking strategically instead of always guessing blind. Now go play for real and make your friends sweat!

walkthrough.title: Mix Truth and Lies Evenly
walkthrough.text: The number one mistake new players make is always lying or always telling the truth. If your opponents figure out your pattern, you are toast. Aim to tell the truth roughly half the time and lie the other half. Keep them guessing! The best Cockroach Poker players are genuinely unpredictable, and the only way to be unpredictable is to actually mix it up.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table, Not Just the Cards
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to what face-up cards everyone has collected. If someone has three Rats in front of them, any time a card is offered with a Rat claim, the whole table changes. That player is in the danger zone, and everyone knows it. Use this information both offensively and defensively.

walkthrough.title: Peek More Than You Think You Should
walkthrough.text: New players love to guess right away because it feels bold and exciting. But peeking is almost always the smarter play when you are unsure. When you peek, you get perfect information and can pass the problem to someone else. The only cost is that one fewer player can receive the card this round. Unless you are very confident in your read, take the peek.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Targets Wisely
walkthrough.text: Do not just offer cards to the person sitting next to you out of habit. Think about who has the most vulnerable face-up collection. Offering a Fly to someone who already has three Flies is devastating. Also consider who is bad at reading bluffs — some players at the table will guess wrong more often, and they make juicy targets.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Own Face-Up Cards
walkthrough.text: Keep track of your own danger zones. If you have three Spiders face-up, you know the table is going to start funneling Spiders your way. When someone offers you a card and claims 'Spider,' your stress level should skyrocket. In these moments, peeking is almost always correct because the stakes of guessing wrong are game-ending.

walkthrough.title: Use the Claim Strategically
walkthrough.text: Remember, when you offer a card, you choose what to claim. You do not have to claim the actual critter type, and you do not have to claim the most plausible type either. Sometimes claiming a type that matches the recipient's weakness creates extra pressure even if the card is something completely different. The claim itself is a weapon.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Hand Size
walkthrough.text: Every card you offer is a card leaving your hand. If your hand runs out and it is your turn to offer, you lose. Be mindful of how many cards you have left, especially in the late game when hands are getting thin. If you are running low, try to win guesses so other people have to keep offering, or peek and pass to avoid being the offerer.

walkthrough.title: Have Fun With It
walkthrough.text: Cockroach Poker is a party game, so lean into the drama! Sell your bluffs with confidence, make exaggerated claims, and celebrate when you catch someone in a lie. The best games happen when everyone is laughing and trash-talking. The critters are gross, the lies are bold, and the betrayals are hilarious. Enjoy the chaos!

summary.title: Mix Truth and Lies Evenly
bullets: Aim for roughly 50/50 truth and lies
Predictable patterns get exploited fast
Being genuinely random is your best defense

summary.title: Read the Table, Not Just the Cards
bullets: Track everyone's face-up cards at all times
3 of a type = danger zone — changes all decisions
Use face-up info offensively and defensively

summary.title: Peek More Than You Think You Should
bullets: Peeking gives perfect information at low cost
Guessing blind is a coin flip — peeking removes the gamble
Only guess when you have a strong read on the offerer

summary.title: Choose Your Targets Wisely
bullets: Target players with 3 face-up cards of a type
Target players who guess wrong often
Don't just default to the player next to you

summary.title: Watch Your Own Face-Up Cards

Peek instead of guessing when the stakes are game-ending
3 of a type face-up = everyone targets you

summary.title: Use the Claim Strategically
bullets: Claim a type matching the recipient's weakness for extra pressure
The claim itself is a weapon — it doesn't have to be truthful or logical
Mismatched claims can confuse opponents about your strategy

summary.title: Manage Your Hand Size
bullets: Empty hand on your offer turn = instant loss
Track your card count in the late game
Win guesses or peek/pass to avoid being the offerer when low

summary.title: Have Fun With It
bullets: Sell your bluffs with confidence and flair
Trash-talk and dramatic reveals make the game shine
The chaos is the point — lean into it!

walkthrough.title: The Double-Truth Gambit
walkthrough.text: Here is a sneaky move that catches experienced players off guard. Offer a card and truthfully name it. When the recipient says 'Yes it is' — correct — and you take it back, offer the exact same type again to the same person next round, still telling the truth. Most players assume you would not tell the truth twice in a row and will switch to 'No it is not.' Wrong! The double-truth exploits the human instinct to expect alternation.

walkthrough.title: Information Warfare
walkthrough.text: Every peek reveals information, and information is power. When you peek at a card and pass it along, you now know one card that is not in your hand or the passer's hand. Track these reveals mentally. If you have peeked at three Scorpions this game, you know there are only five left in play. This knowledge helps you evaluate future claims — if someone says 'This is a Scorpion' but you have already tracked most of them, you can make much better guesses.

walkthrough.title: The Poison Pass
walkthrough.text: When you peek at a card and see it matches a critter type that your target already has three of, you have a devastating weapon. Pass it to that player and claim the matching type. Now they are in an impossible position. If they believe you and say 'Yes it is' and they are wrong, they take a random card. But if it really is that type and they say 'No it is not' — game over. The poison pass forces your opponents into lose-lose scenarios.

walkthrough.title: Reading Behavioral Patterns
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to how each player behaves when they are lying versus telling the truth. Some players talk more when bluffing, some get quieter. Some make eye contact when lying, others avoid it. Some players have a nervous laugh that only appears with a bluff. These tells are subtle, but over the course of a game, patterns emerge. Once you have a read on someone, you can exploit it mercilessly.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Peeking Chains
walkthrough.text: When you are in the danger zone with three of a type, your survival depends on never guessing wrong on that type. Always peek when offered a card claimed as your danger type. After peeking, pass it to someone who is not in danger — spread the risk around. If you peek and discover it really is your danger type, pass it to another player claiming a completely different critter. This throws off their read and keeps you alive.

walkthrough.title: Hand Count Awareness
walkthrough.text: In the late game, card counts matter enormously. If an opponent has only two or three cards left in hand, they are running low. Every offer they make shrinks their hand further. You can exploit this by making them the offerer repeatedly — force guesses that they lose so they have to keep offering with a dwindling hand. Conversely, if your hand is thin, peek and pass whenever possible to avoid being the one who has to offer.

walkthrough.title: The Reverse Psychology Claim
walkthrough.text: Advanced players know that claiming your opponent's danger type when the card is actually something else is a common move. So go one level deeper. When you actually have their danger type, claim it honestly. They will think 'Nobody would actually offer me the card I am about to lose to — this must be a bluff.' They say 'No it is not,' the card flips, and they are out. Layered bluffing is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Alliance and Betrayal
walkthrough.text: Even though Cockroach Poker has no official teams, informal alliances form naturally. Players with clean boards might team up against the player in the danger zone. Use this to your advantage. Let the table gang up on someone else while you fly under the radar. But be ready to betray an ally the moment it benefits you. There is only one loser in this game, and the goal is to make sure it is not you.

summary.title: The Double-Truth Gambit
bullets: Tell the truth twice in a row to the same player
Exploits the human instinct to expect alternation
Most players assume you'd switch to a lie after telling truth

summary.title: Information Warfare
bullets: Track every card you peek at — build a mental count
8 cards per type; knowing where they are improves your guesses
Information from peeks compounds over the game

summary.title: The Poison Pass
bullets: Peek → see a card matching someone's danger type → pass it to them
Claim the matching type to create a lose-lose guess
Forces opponents into game-ending pressure

summary.title: Reading Behavioral Patterns
bullets: Watch for tells: talkativeness, eye contact, nervous habits
Patterns emerge over multiple rounds — track them
Exploit reliable tells mercilessly

summary.title: Defensive Peeking Chains
bullets: Always peek when offered your danger type — never guess blind
Pass danger cards to safe players with a misleading claim
Survival = never guessing wrong on your 3-of-a-kind type

summary.title: Hand Count Awareness
bullets: Track opponents' approximate hand sizes
Force low-hand players into offerer role repeatedly
Protect your own hand by peeking/passing instead of offering

summary.title: The Reverse Psychology Claim
bullets: Honestly claim the danger type when you actually have it
Opponents assume 'nobody would really offer me that' → call bluff → lose
Layered bluffing: go deeper than the expected meta-game

summary.title: Alliance and Betrayal
bullets: Informal alliances form naturally — let others gang up on the leader
Fly under the radar while attention is elsewhere
Be ready to betray allies the moment it benefits you

entries.definition: One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Cockroach cards total. Shown as a brown cockroach illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Bat cards total. Shown as a dark bat illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Fly cards total. Shown as a green fly illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Toad cards total. Shown as a warty toad illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Rat cards total. Shown as a gray rat illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Scorpion cards total. Shown as a red scorpion illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Spider cards total. Shown as a black spider illustration on the card face.
One of the eight critter types in the deck. There are eight Stink Bug cards total. Shown as a green stink bug illustration on the card face.

The act of placing a card face-down in front of another player and claiming a critter type. The offerer chooses any card from their hand and may claim any of the eight types, regardless of what the card actually is.
The verbal declaration made when offering or passing a card. The claim names one of the eight critter types. It can be truthful or a bluff — the recipient must determine which.

One of the two responses when receiving an offered card. The recipient secretly looks at the card without showing anyone else, then must pass it face-down to another player who has not seen it this round, accompanied by a new claim.

After peeking, the act of sliding the card face-down to a new recipient with a claim. You cannot pass the card back to anyone who has already seen it during the current round, including the original offerer.

A card placed face-up in front of a player after they lose a guess. Face-up cards remain in front of that player for the rest of the game and are visible to everyone. Collecting four face-up cards of the same critter type causes that player to lose.
The primary loss condition. If a player ever has four face-up cards showing the same critter type in front of them, they immediately lose and the game ends. All other players win.
The secondary loss condition. If it is a player's turn to make an offer but they have zero cards remaining in their hand, they immediately lose and the game ends. All other players win.
